This may be the best time in history to start a business.  But if before the bottleneck was building the product, now the bottleneck is knowing what to build. And these lists are super cool because we have several people at IC who are incredibly tech- savvy, investors, people who work in Silicon Valley, and they are super up-to-date on the latest AI trends. These lists, if we take the summer 2026 and spring lists here, are quite extensive.  Well, there are even some very technical topics there, but I'm going to try to bring you a curated selection of the best ideas I've found that are a bit more democratic for you to implement, things that aren't hardware-related, requiring a lot of capital expenditure to get started, or things that are perhaps too much like space science, which maybe not everyone will be able to do.  I'm going to share with you six business opportunities that I liked the most and that are relatively easy for you to get started.  The first opportunity I brought up here is what we're calling AI Native services.  So, what is this, okay? AI Native is a service company, but it's a company entirely powered by AI.  So yes, you will provide a service, you will sell a service, but the way you deliver that service is all handled by EAI.  And why is this a good deal?  And why is this a paradigm shift compared to what other people were building? I'll explain it to you here. We have two ways of looking at this, okay?  We can take something, automate that thing, and sell that automation as a SaaS (Software for Allergy).  Or we could automate this and sell it as a service.  In other words, I'll take this automation that I've created and use it for myself, so I can deliver a better service than my competitors.  So, to add a little more detail and be a bit more practical about these two paths I've shown you, okay?  If we're going to go down this path of automating something and selling it as SaaS, let's take an example, let's say that something is accounting, okay?  So, I'm someone who is very knowledgeable about accounting, and I want to automate the entire accounting process. Beauty?  If I were to go the SAS route, I would create a SAS for accountants and sell it to accountants.  In other words, my client is the accountant.  If I go down the path of selling as a service, I'll automate accounting and sell accounting services to the end customer who hires accountants.  In other words, my current client will become my competitor in this other option here.  And that's why I think that's brilliant, okay?  And it's even much better than creating a SAS for numerous reasons.  To begin with, because the services market is much larger than the SAS market.  So, to make this a little easier to understand, think about it this way: how much does your company spend on CRM licenses, for example?  Then you save that information.  So you might think : "How much do I spend in my company paying the salaries of salespeople, who are the CRM users?" I don't know what number you'll arrive at, but it will certainly be much higher than the amount you pay to your CRM provider. To make it a little more tangible, to give you an idea of how big the service market is versus SAS, we're talking about a $17 trillion market versus a $465 billion market. When you look at those $465 billion, it doesn't seem like a small market, but when you compare it to $17 trillion, it seems like a very, very small market. So, what we're saying is that you should position your company as a service provider, even if you have all the technological infrastructure behind you to deliver that service. Position it as a service provider because then you'll be targeting the $17 trillion market, which is roughly, I don't know how to do the math here, but maybe about 20 times larger than the market, right?  So it's much, much, much bigger, and then you have the chance to build a much, much bigger business as well. The second advantage is the issue of adoption risk, because think about it , a SaaS that nobody uses will be canceled, and then you'll have a churn risk, right? Because if you sold a SaaS and nobody is using it, at some point someone will want to cancel it. When you provide the service, you're not delivering a job to your client. So you're guaranteeing that they will benefit from your services, and the chance of them canceling is much lower. The other advantage is that you can charge more because you 're not just selling a software license, you're selling the result, right? You're selling, in the case of the accounting example, all the client's accounting; they won't need to worry about it anymore, you'll take care of it. So you're not just selling a service, so it's different. You could charge maybe R$100 per month for a SaaS and R$ 2,000 for a service, right? So you can charge much more, and that will allow you to...  Have better margins, because you realize that if you charge 2,000 and you have a competitor, an accountant who also charges 2,000 , the money you'll put in your pocket is much more than your competitor will put in their pocket.
